2564	\section{Pullback-monoidality: invertibility under a general scheme morphism}
2565	\label{sec:tensorobj_pullback_monoidality}
2566	
2567	The relative Picard consumer (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}) re-bases its
2568	line-bundle carrier onto the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
2569	\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}, and its
2570	functorial structure maps are \emph{pullback} maps of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules: the
2571	projection pullback \(\pi_T^*\) along \(C \times_S T \to T\) and the base-change
2572	pullback along \(C \times_S T' \to C \times_S T\). For these maps to descend to the
2573	\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) carrier one needs that \emph{pullback preserves
2574	\(\otimes\)-invertibility} for \emph{general} morphisms of schemes --- the
2575	projection and the base-change morphism are neither open immersions nor flat. This
2576	is the target lemma \cref{lem:isinvertible_pullback} of the section.
2577	
2578	This is a genuinely different fact from the open-immersion compatibility
2579	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, and not a corollary of it. That lemma handles
2580	restriction along an open immersion, and it does so by routing through the
2581	\emph{lax right} adjoint \(\mathtt{pushforward}/\mathtt{restrictScalars}\), which is
2582	strong monoidal there \emph{only} because an open immersion's structure-sheaf ring
2583	map is a local \emph{isomorphism} (\(f.\mathtt{appIso}\)). A general scheme
2584	morphism has no such isomorphism, so the right-adjoint route is unavailable.
2585	
2586	\paragraph{The general comparison is descoped.} One might attempt
2587	\cref{lem:isinvertible_pullback} through the \emph{general} pullback--tensor
2588	comparison \(f^*(M \otimes_X N) \cong f^*M \otimes_Y f^*N\) for \emph{arbitrary}
2589	\(M, N\) (\cref{lem:pullback_tensor_iso}). Mathematically that isomorphism is true
2590	for every morphism --- pullback is extension of scalars and extension of scalars is
2591	strong monoidal --- but \emph{formalising the general iso is Mathlib-scale and is not
2592	needed on any project path}. The obstruction is structural: the project carries
2593	\(f^*\) \emph{abstractly}, as the left adjoint
2594	\((\mathtt{pushforward}\,f).\mathtt{leftAdjoint}\), with no sectionwise value to which
2595	one could attach the extension-of-scalars tensorator. Building the comparison as an
2596	isomorphism by the concrete-model route would require a concrete model \(P =
2597	\mathtt{sheafification} \circ (\text{presheaf base change})\), and that model needs
2598	two ingredients absent from Mathlib at the pinned commit: a presheaf-level
2599	extension-of-scalars functor \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.extendScalars}\), and a
2600	topological inverse-image functor (a left Kan extension along
2601	\((\mathtt{Opens.map}\,f.\mathtt{base}).\mathrm{op}\)). The Stacks proof of the
2602	general statement is itself recorded as ``Omitted''
2603	(\cref{lem:pullback_tensor_iso}): it is standard mathematics with no slick
2604	formalisation. We therefore \emph{descope} the general iso and prove only the
2605	invertible case, which is all the downstream consumers (relative Picard functor,
2606	Quot embedding, tangent computation, Albanese universal property) ever require.
2607	
2608	\paragraph{The route: local trivialisation of the invertible pair.} For invertible
2609	\(M, N\) the comparison map \(\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}} : f^*(M \otimes_X N) \to f^*M
2610	\otimes_Y f^*N\) (\cref{lem:pullback_tensor_map}) \emph{is} an isomorphism, and we
2611	prove this not in general but on the specific invertible pair, by local
2612	trivialisation. The comparison map itself exists for arbitrary \(M, N\): it is the
2613	sheaf-level transport of the presheaf-level oplax comparison
2614	(\cref{lem:presheaf_pullback_oplaxmonoidal}) through sheafification, and it sidesteps
2615	the absent inverse image because it is only a \emph{map}, not an iso. Invertibility
2616	enters only to upgrade that map to an iso: an invertible module is locally free of
2617	rank \(1\) (\cref{lem:isinvertible_implies_locallytrivial}), so \(M, N\), and hence
2618	\(M \otimes_X N\), become \(\mathcal{O}_X\) on a common affine cover; their pullbacks
2619	become \(\mathcal{O}_Y\) on the preimage cover (\(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial.pullback}\)),
2620	where \(\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}}\) restricts to the canonical comparison
2621	\(\mathcal{O} \otimes \mathcal{O} \cong \mathcal{O}\), an isomorphism; and a morphism
2622	of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules that is an isomorphism after restriction to every member
2623	of a cover is an isomorphism (\cref{lem:isiso_of_isiso_restrict}).
2624	
2625	\paragraph{Two negative results that pin the route.} First, one might hope that since
2626	pullback is \emph{some} (op)lax monoidal functor, invertible objects are preserved for
2627	free. This is \emph{false}: global sections \(\Gamma\) is lax monoidal, yet
2628	\(\Gamma(\mathbb{P}^1, \mathcal{O}(1)) = 0\) is not an invertible module, so a mere
2629	oplax comparison map does \emph{not} suffice --- one genuinely has to prove the tensor
2630	comparison is an \emph{isomorphism}. This is exactly why
2631	\cref{lem:isinvertible_pullback} cannot be read off the presheaf-level oplax structure
2632	of \cref{lem:presheaf_pullback_oplaxmonoidal} alone, and must localise.
2633	
2634	Second --- and crucially --- the local-trivialisation route is \emph{not} the shelved
2635	``invertible \(\Rightarrow\) locally \(\mathcal{O}\) \(\Rightarrow\) recover
2636	invertibility from local triviality'' mirage. That mirage is the \emph{reverse} bridge
2637	\(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial} \Rightarrow \mathtt{IsInvertible}\), the dual/tensor-inverse
2638	manufacture that the invertibility-carrier pivot (\cref{sec:tensorobj_pic_carrier}) was
2639	built to delete; it stays off-path here. Our route never recovers invertibility from
2640	local triviality: it produces the explicit witness \(f^*N\) directly and uses local
2641	triviality \emph{only} in the \emph{forward} direction
2642	\(\mathtt{IsInvertible} \Rightarrow \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\)
2643	(\cref{lem:isinvertible_implies_locallytrivial}, invertible \(\Rightarrow\) locally free
2644	of rank \(1\), which is genuinely available), and only to certify that the comparison
2645	map \(\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}}\) is an isomorphism on that one pair.
2646	
2647	\paragraph{Guardrail.} The iso-ness of \(\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}}\) must be checked
2648	\emph{on a cover} via \cref{lem:isiso_of_isiso_restrict}, \emph{not} stalkwise.
2649	Reducing it to stalks would re-derive the varying-ring stalk-tensor commutation; the cover route avoids that
2650	entirely, because on each trivialising open all three modules are literally
2651	\(\mathcal{O}\) and the comparison is the canonical \(\mathcal{O} \otimes \mathcal{O}
2652	\cong \mathcal{O}\).

\paragraph{The general comparison is descoped.} One might attempt
\cref{lem:isinvertible_pullback} through the \emph{general} pullback--tensor
comparison \(f^*(M \otimes_X N) \cong f^*M \otimes_Y f^*N\) for \emph{arbitrary}
\(M, N\) (\cref{lem:pullback_tensor_iso}). Mathematically that isomorphism is true
for every morphism --- pullback is extension of scalars and extension of scalars is
strong monoidal --- but \emph{formalising the general iso is Mathlib-scale and is not
needed on any project path}. The obstruction is structural: the project carries
\(f^*\) \emph{abstractly}, as the left adjoint
\((\mathtt{pushforward}\,f).\mathtt{leftAdjoint}\), with no sectionwise value to which
one could attach the extension-of-scalars tensorator. Building the comparison as an
isomorphism by the concrete-model route would require a concrete model \(P =
\mathtt{sheafification} \circ (\text{presheaf base change})\), and that model needs
two ingredients absent from Mathlib at the pinned commit: a presheaf-level
extension-of-scalars functor \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.extendScalars}\), and a
topological inverse-image functor (a left Kan extension along
\((\mathtt{Opens.map}\,f.\mathtt{base}).\mathrm{op}\)). The Stacks proof of the
general statement is itself recorded as ``Omitted''
(\cref{lem:pullback_tensor_iso}): it is standard mathematics with no slick
formalisation. We therefore \emph{descope} the general iso and prove only the
invertible case, which is all the downstream consumers (relative Picard functor,
Quot embedding, tangent computation, Albanese universal property) ever require.
input.new_string:
\paragraph{The reduction to two general facts.} The target
\cref{lem:isinvertible_pullback} is a three-line consequence of two facts about
pullback, exactly as in the Stacks proof of \texttt{lemma-pullback-invertible}. The
first is that pullback commutes with the tensor product for \emph{all}
\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules: the canonical comparison
\(f^*(M \otimes_X N) \cong f^*M \otimes_Y f^*N\) is an isomorphism for every \(M, N\)
and every \(f\) (\cref{lem:pullback_tensor_iso}, the general strong-monoidality of
pullback). The second is that pullback preserves the structure sheaf,
\(f^*\mathcal{O}_X \cong \mathcal{O}_Y\) (\cref{lem:pullback_unit_iso}, already in
hand). Given an inverse \(N\) with \(M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\), pulling back
yields
\[
  f^*M \otimes_Y f^*N
    \;\cong\; f^*(M \otimes_X N)
    \;\cong\; f^*\mathcal{O}_X
    \;\cong\; \mathcal{O}_Y,
\]
so \(f^*N\) is a tensor inverse of \(f^*M\). The substrate therefore \emph{commits} to
building the general comparison \cref{lem:pullback_tensor_iso} as a genuine
isomorphism; it is the load-bearing input, and every downstream consumer (relative
Picard functor, Quot embedding, tangent computation, Albanese universal property) is
served by it.

\paragraph{The route: local trivialisation of the invertible pair.} For invertible
\(M, N\) the comparison map \(\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}} : f^*(M \otimes_X N) \to f^*M
\otimes_Y f^*N\) (\cref{lem:pullback_tensor_map}) \emph{is} an isomorphism, and we
prove this not in general but on the specific invertible pair, by local
trivialisation. The comparison map itself exists for arbitrary \(M, N\): it is the
sheaf-level transport of the presheaf-level oplax comparison
(\cref{lem:presheaf_pullback_oplaxmonoidal}) through sheafification, and it sidesteps
the absent inverse image because it is only a \emph{map}, not an iso. Invertibility
enters only to upgrade that map to an iso: an invertible module is locally free of
rank \(1\) (\cref{lem:isinvertible_implies_locallytrivial}), so \(M, N\), and hence
\(M \otimes_X N\), become \(\mathcal{O}_X\) on a common affine cover; their pullbacks
become \(\mathcal{O}_Y\) on the preimage cover (\(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial.pullback}\)),
where \(\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}}\) restricts to the canonical comparison
\(\mathcal{O} \otimes \mathcal{O} \cong \mathcal{O}\), an isomorphism; and a morphism
of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules that is an isomorphism after restriction to every member
of a cover is an isomorphism (\cref{lem:isiso_of_isiso_restrict}).
input.new_string:
\paragraph{Why the general isomorphism is genuine content, not free.} The comparison is
\emph{not} a formal consequence of pullback being \emph{some} (op)lax monoidal functor.
An (op)lax monoidal functor need not preserve invertible objects: global sections
\(\Gamma\) is lax monoidal, yet \(\Gamma(\mathbb{P}^1, \mathcal{O}(1)) = 0\) is not an
invertible module, so the bare oplax comparison \emph{map} carries no invertibility
information. One must genuinely prove that the comparison \(\delta\) is an
\emph{isomorphism}. This is precisely why \cref{lem:pullback_tensor_iso} is real work
and not a corollary of the oplax comparison map
\cref{lem:presheaf_pullback_oplaxmonoidal}: the oplax structure supplies \(\delta\) as a
morphism for free (\cref{lem:pullback_tensor_map}), but its invertibility is the entire
content.

\paragraph{Two negative results that pin the route.} First, one might hope that since
pullback is \emph{some} (op)lax monoidal functor, invertible objects are preserved for
free. This is \emph{false}: global sections \(\Gamma\) is lax monoidal, yet
\(\Gamma(\mathbb{P}^1, \mathcal{O}(1)) = 0\) is not an invertible module, so a mere
oplax comparison map does \emph{not} suffice --- one genuinely has to prove the tensor
comparison is an \emph{isomorphism}. This is exactly why
\cref{lem:isinvertible_pullback} cannot be read off the presheaf-level oplax structure
of \cref{lem:presheaf_pullback_oplaxmonoidal} alone, and must localise.

Second --- and crucially --- the local-trivialisation route is \emph{not} the shelved
``invertible \(\Rightarrow\) locally \(\mathcal{O}\) \(\Rightarrow\) recover
invertibility from local triviality'' mirage. That mirage is the \emph{reverse} bridge
\(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial} \Rightarrow \mathtt{IsInvertible}\), the dual/tensor-inverse
manufacture that the invertibility-carrier pivot (\cref{sec:tensorobj_pic_carrier}) was
built to delete; it stays off-path here. Our route never recovers invertibility from
local triviality: it produces the explicit witness \(f^*N\) directly and uses local
triviality \emph{only} in the \emph{forward} direction
\(\mathtt{IsInvertible} \Rightarrow \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\)
(\cref{lem:isinvertible_implies_locallytrivial}, invertible \(\Rightarrow\) locally free
of rank \(1\), which is genuinely available), and only to certify that the comparison
map \(\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}}\) is an isomorphism on that one pair.

\paragraph{Guardrail.} The iso-ness of \(\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}}\) must be checked
\emph{on a cover} via \cref{lem:isiso_of_isiso_restrict}, \emph{not} stalkwise.
Reducing it to stalks would re-derive the varying-ring stalk-tensor commutation; the cover route avoids that
entirely, because on each trivialising open all three modules are literally
\(\mathcal{O}\) and the comparison is the canonical \(\mathcal{O} \otimes \mathcal{O}
\cong \mathcal{O}\).
input.new_string:
\paragraph{The committed construction route.} The isomorphism is built on a
\emph{concrete} model of the pullback and transported to the project's abstract
\(f^* = (\mathtt{pushforward}\,f).\mathtt{leftAdjoint}\) along the uniqueness of left
adjoints --- the same \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\) device that closed
\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}. The construction decomposes into four steps, carried
out in the proof of \cref{lem:pullback_tensor_iso} and the sub-lemmas it cites:
\begin{itemize}
  \item[(D1)] \emph{Decomposition.} The presheaf pushforward factors as a fixed-ring
    pushforward followed by restriction of scalars, so its left adjoint --- the
    presheaf pullback --- factors as a topological inverse image \(\mathtt{pullback}_0\)
    followed by extension of scalars (\cref{lem:pullback_lan_decomposition}).
  \item[(D2)] \emph{Scalar half is strong (free).} Extension of scalars is strong
    monoidal via the base-change distributivity isomorphism
    \(\mathtt{TensorProduct.AlgebraTensorModule.distribBaseChange}\); so the comparison
    \(\delta\) for the pullback is an isomorphism \emph{if and only if} the comparison
    \(\delta_0\) for \(\mathtt{pullback}_0\) is.
  \item[(D3)] \emph{Topological half --- the genuine build.} \(\mathtt{pullback}_0\) is
    the left Kan extension along \((\mathtt{Opens.map}\,f.\mathtt{base})^{\mathrm{op}}\);
    pointwise its value at an open \(V\) is a colimit over the comma category
    \((F \downarrow V) = \{U : f^{-1}V \subseteq U\}\). This category is up-directed
    (\(U_1 \cup U_2\) is an upper bound), and the tensor product commutes with filtered
    colimits, so \(\delta_0\) is an isomorphism (\cref{lem:pullback0_tensor_iso}).
  \item[(D4)] \emph{Sheafify and transport.} Sheafify the resulting presheaf-level
    isomorphism --- the unit and tensor factors are reconciled with their sheafified
    forms by the sheafification-monoidality brick \(\mathtt{sheafifyTensorUnitIso}\) ---
    then transport the strong-monoidal structure from the concrete model to
    \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\) along \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\),
    yielding \cref{lem:pullback_tensor_iso}.
\end{itemize}
The honest cost is concentrated in (D3): the filtered-colimit/tensor interchange for
\(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\)-valued presheaves and the concrete pointwise model of
\(\mathtt{pullback}_0\) are absent from Mathlib at the pinned commit and are the
multi-hundred-line content, built bottom-up. The remaining inputs ---
\(\mathtt{distribBaseChange}\), \(\mathtt{leftAdjointUniq}\),
\(\mathtt{sheafifyTensorUnitIso}\), and the comparison \emph{map}
\cref{lem:pullback_tensor_map} --- are already in hand.

  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso,
        lem:presheaf_pullback_oplaxmonoidal}
  We record the would-be concrete-model proof to document where it stalls; the statement

  Mathematically, pullback is extension of scalars and extension of scalars is strong
  monoidal, so the comparison is an isomorphism for every \(f\). The only work is to
  carry this through the formalisation's \emph{abstract} pullback \(f^* =
  (\mathtt{pushforward}\,f).\mathtt{leftAdjoint}\), which has no sectionwise value. We
  do not compute \(f^*\) sectionwise; instead we equip a \emph{concrete} model of the
  pullback with a strong-monoidal structure and transport it to \(f^*\) along the
  uniqueness of left adjoints. This mirrors Mathlib's own construction of the
  extension-of-scalars monoidal structure in
  \texttt{Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Monoidal/Adjunction.lean}, where the
  \emph{left} adjoint's strong-monoidal structure is built explicitly (via the
  base-change distributivity isomorphism) and only afterwards is the right adjoint's
  lax structure derived from the adjunction --- never the reverse direction. The
  argument has three steps.

  \emph{Step 1 (a concrete strong-monoidal pullback \(P\)).} Build the concrete functor
  \[

  \]

  site --- the same \(\mathtt{sheafificationCompPullback}\) shape already used in
  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}. Equip \(P\) with a strong-monoidal structure. Its
  tensorator is the linear-algebra strong-monoidal core
  \(\mathtt{TensorProduct.AlgebraTensorModule.distribBaseChange}\),
  \[
    (M \otimes_R N) \otimes_R S
      \;\cong\;
    (M \otimes_R S) \otimes_S (N \otimes_R S),
  \]
  an isomorphism for \emph{every} ring map \(R \to S\), with no flatness, sheafified
  pointwise; this is precisely Mathlib's \((\mathtt{extendScalars}\,\varphi).\mathtt{Monoidal}\)
  lifted to sheaves. The unit comparison is the already-landed sheafification-monoidality
  brick \(\mathtt{sheafifyTensorUnitIso}\). Thus \(P\) is strong monoidal with each
  tensorator and the unit comparison an isomorphism, with no hypothesis on \(f\).

  \emph{Step 2 (\(P\) is left adjoint to pushforward).} Exhibit an adjunction
  \(P \dashv \mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforward}\,f\). This is the universal property
  of sheafified extension of scalars: the presheaf base change is left adjoint to
  presheaf restriction of scalars, and sheafification is left adjoint to the inclusion,

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso,
        lem:pullback_lan_decomposition, lem:pullback0_tensor_iso,
        lem:pullback_tensor_map}
  Mathematically, pullback is extension of scalars and extension of scalars is strong
  monoidal, so the comparison is an isomorphism for every \(f\). The only work is to
  carry this through the formalisation's \emph{abstract} pullback
  \(f^* = (\mathtt{pushforward}\,f).\mathtt{leftAdjoint}\), which exposes no sectionwise
  value. We do not evaluate \(f^*\) sectionwise; we equip a \emph{concrete} model of the
  pullback with a strong-monoidal structure and transport it to \(f^*\) along the
  uniqueness of left adjoints. This mirrors Mathlib's own construction of the
  extension-of-scalars monoidal structure in
  \texttt{Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Monoidal/Adjunction.lean}, where the
  \emph{left} adjoint's strong-monoidal structure is built explicitly (via the
  base-change distributivity isomorphism) and the right adjoint's lax structure is
  derived from the adjunction --- never the reverse direction. The argument has four
  steps.

  \emph{Step 1 (D1: decomposition).} The presheaf pushforward factors as a fixed-ring
  pushforward followed by restriction of scalars,
  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi = \mathtt{pushforward}_0\,F\,R \circ
  \mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\). Taking left adjoints reverses the composite, so the
  presheaf pullback factors as
  \[
    \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi \;\cong\; \mathtt{extendScalars}\,\varphi \circ
    \mathtt{pullback}_0, \qquad
    \mathtt{pullback}_0 = (\mathtt{pushforward}_0\,F\,R).\mathtt{leftAdjoint},
  \]
  with \(\mathtt{pullback}_0\) the topological inverse image
  (\cref{lem:pullback_lan_decomposition}).

  \emph{Step 2 (D2: the scalar half is strong, for free).} Extension of scalars
  \(\mathtt{extendScalars}\,\varphi\) is strong monoidal: its tensorator is the
  base-change distributivity isomorphism
  \(\mathtt{TensorProduct.AlgebraTensorModule.distribBaseChange}\),
  \[
    (M \otimes_R N) \otimes_R S
      \;\cong\;
    (M \otimes_R S) \otimes_S (N \otimes_R S),
  \]
  an isomorphism for \emph{every} ring map \(R \to S\), with no flatness. Composing a
  strong-monoidal functor with \(\mathtt{pullback}_0\), the comparison \(\delta\) for
  \(\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\) is therefore an isomorphism \emph{if and only if} the
  comparison \(\delta_0\) for \(\mathtt{pullback}_0\) is. This isolates the topological
  half as the sole genuine obligation.

  \emph{Step 3 (D3: the topological half --- the genuine build).} The functor
  \(\mathtt{pullback}_0\) is the left Kan extension
  \(\mathrm{Lan}\,(\mathtt{Opens.map}\,f.\mathtt{base})^{\mathrm{op}}\) on underlying
  presheaves (its right adjoint \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\) is restriction along
  \(F^{\mathrm{op}}\)). Pointwise, with \(F = (\mathtt{Opens.map}\,f.\mathtt{base})^{\mathrm{op}}\),
  \[
    \mathtt{pullback}_0(M \otimes N)(V)
      = \operatorname*{colim}_{(F \downarrow V)} \bigl(M(U) \otimes N(U)\bigr),
    \quad
    (\mathtt{pullback}_0 M \otimes \mathtt{pullback}_0 N)(V)
      = \Bigl(\operatorname*{colim} M(U)\Bigr) \otimes \Bigl(\operatorname*{colim} N(U)\Bigr).
  \]
  The comma category \((F \downarrow V) = \{U : f^{-1}V \subseteq U\}\) is up-directed
  --- \(U_1 \cup U_2\) is an upper bound of any \(U_1, U_2\) --- and the tensor product
  commutes with filtered colimits (the diagonal \((F\downarrow V) \to (F\downarrow V)
  \times (F \downarrow V)\) is final). Hence the comparison \(\delta_0\) is an
  isomorphism (\cref{lem:pullback0_tensor_iso}). This is the genuine, Mathlib-absent
  content of the lemma.

  \emph{Step 4 (D4: sheafify and transport).} Steps 1--3 furnish a strong-monoidal
  structure on the concrete presheaf-level inverse image. Sheafify the resulting presheaf
  isomorphism to the sheaf level --- the unit and tensor factors are reconciled with
  their sheafified forms by the sheafification-monoidality brick
  \(\mathtt{sheafifyTensorUnitIso}\), the same \(\mathtt{sheafificationCompPullback}\)
  shape used in \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}. By definition
  \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\,f = (\mathtt{pushforward}\,f).\mathtt{leftAdjoint}\)
  is \emph{a} left adjoint of the pushforward; the uniqueness of left adjoints
  \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\) supplies a canonical natural isomorphism from it
  to the concrete model, along which the strong-monoidal structure travels. Its
  tensorator becomes the asserted isomorphism
  \[
    f^*(M \otimes_X N)
      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
    (f^*M) \otimes_Y (f^*N),
  \]
  natural in \(M\) and \(N\), refining the comparison map
  \(\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}} = \mathtt{pullbackTensorMap}\) of
  \cref{lem:pullback_tensor_map}; the abstract pullback is never evaluated sectionwise.

  No shortcut from the bare oplax map is available: the presheaf pullback is oplax
  monoidal (\cref{lem:presheaf_pullback_oplaxmonoidal}) and so supplies \(\delta\) as a
  morphism for free, but an (op)lax monoidal functor does not preserve invertible objects
  --- global sections \(\Gamma\) is lax monoidal yet \(\Gamma(\mathbb{P}^1,
  \mathcal{O}(1)) = 0\) is not invertible --- so the isomorphism must be proved, which is
  exactly the work of Steps~1--4.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
[Presheaf pullback factors as topological inverse image then extension of scalars]
  \label{lem:pullback_lan_decomposition}
  \uses{lem:presheaf_pushforward_laxmonoidal}
  Let \(\varphi\) present a pushforward of presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along a
  functor \(F\) of ring presheaves. The presheaf pushforward factors as the fixed-ring
  pushforward followed by restriction of scalars,
  \[
    \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pushforward}\,\varphi
      \;=\;
    \mathtt{pushforward}_0\,F\,R \circ \mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi .
  \]
  Consequently its left adjoint, the presheaf pullback, factors as
  \[
    \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi
      \;\cong\;
    \mathtt{extendScalars}\,\varphi \circ \mathtt{pullback}_0,
    \qquad
    \mathtt{pullback}_0 := (\mathtt{pushforward}_0\,F\,R).\mathtt{leftAdjoint},
  \]
  where \(\mathtt{pullback}_0\) is the topological inverse image --- the left Kan
  extension along \((\mathtt{Opens.map}\,f.\mathtt{base})^{\mathrm{op}}\) --- and
  \(\mathtt{extendScalars}\,\varphi\) is sectionwise extension of scalars along the
  structure-sheaf ring maps.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:presheaf_pushforward_laxmonoidal}
  The pushforward factorisation is the definitional decomposition of the presheaf
  pushforward into a fixed-ring pushforward and a restriction of scalars, the same
  decomposition that supplies the lax structure of
  \cref{lem:presheaf_pushforward_laxmonoidal}. The left adjoint of a composite is the
  composite of the left adjoints in the reverse order; the left adjoint of
  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) is \(\mathtt{extendScalars}\,\varphi\), and the
  left adjoint of \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\,F\,R\) is by definition
  \(\mathtt{pullback}_0\). The displayed isomorphism is the resulting decomposition of the
  pullback, canonical because each factor's adjoint is unique up to canonical
  isomorphism. That \(\mathtt{pullback}_0\) is the left Kan extension along
  \((\mathtt{Opens.map}\,f.\mathtt{base})^{\mathrm{op}}\) follows because its right adjoint
  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\) is, on underlying presheaves, restriction along that functor.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
[The topological inverse image commutes with $\otimes$ (filtered-colimit interchange)]
  \label{lem:pullback0_tensor_iso}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullback0TensorIso}
  \uses{lem:pullback_lan_decomposition}
  Let \(f : Y \to X\) be a morphism of schemes and let \(\mathtt{pullback}_0\) be the
  topological inverse image of presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules of
  \cref{lem:pullback_lan_decomposition} --- the left Kan extension along
  \((\mathtt{Opens.map}\,f.\mathtt{base})^{\mathrm{op}}\). For arbitrary presheaves of
  modules \(M, N\), the canonical comparison
  \[
    \delta_0 :
    \mathtt{pullback}_0(M \otimes N)
      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
    \mathtt{pullback}_0 M \otimes \mathtt{pullback}_0 N
  \]
  is an isomorphism, natural in \(M, N\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:pullback_lan_decomposition}
  Evaluate both sides pointwise. As a left Kan extension, \(\mathtt{pullback}_0\) has the
  pointwise colimit formula
  \[
    \mathtt{pullback}_0(P)(V)
      \;=\;
    \operatorname*{colim}_{(F \downarrow V)} P(U),
    \qquad F = (\mathtt{Opens.map}\,f.\mathtt{base})^{\mathrm{op}},
  \]
  over the comma category \((F \downarrow V)\), whose objects are the opens \(U\) of
  \(X\) with \(f^{-1}V \subseteq U\). Since the presheaf tensor product is sectionwise,
  \((M \otimes N)(U) = M(U) \otimes N(U)\), one has
  \[
    \mathtt{pullback}_0(M \otimes N)(V)
      = \operatorname*{colim}_{(F \downarrow V)} \bigl(M(U) \otimes N(U)\bigr),
    \quad
    \bigl(\mathtt{pullback}_0 M \otimes \mathtt{pullback}_0 N\bigr)(V)
      = \Bigl(\operatorname*{colim} M(U)\Bigr) \otimes \Bigl(\operatorname*{colim} N(U)\Bigr),
  \]
  and \(\delta_0(V)\) is the canonical comparison between them. The comma category
  \((F \downarrow V)\) is up-directed: it is nonempty (the whole space \(X\) contains
  \(f^{-1}V\)) and any two members \(U_1, U_2\) have the upper bound \(U_1 \cup U_2\),
  still containing \(f^{-1}V\). Over a filtered (here up-directed) index the tensor
  product commutes with the colimit --- the diagonal \((F\downarrow V) \to (F\downarrow V)
  \times (F\downarrow V)\) is final, so the colimit of the pointwise tensors agrees with
  the tensor of the colimits --- whence \(\delta_0(V)\) is an isomorphism for every \(V\),
  and \(\delta_0\) is an isomorphism of presheaves, natural in \(M, N\).

  The pointwise left-Kan-extension model of \(\mathtt{pullback}_0\) and the
  filtered-colimit/tensor interchange for \(\mathcal{O}\)-module-valued presheaves are not
  available in Mathlib at the pinned commit; this lemma is the multi-hundred-line content
  of the build and is established bottom-up, from the left-Kan-extension colimit formula
  together with the filtered-colimit preservation of the tensor product. The argument
  above records the mathematical structure of that build, not a one-line derivation.
\end{proof}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible, lem:pullback_tensor_iso, lem:pullback_unit_iso}
  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Lemma \texttt{lemma-pullback-invertible}.}
  This is the Stacks proof in the project's notation, using the general comparison
  \cref{lem:pullback_tensor_iso} directly --- no local trivialisation, no cover.

  By \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)\) (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}) there is a
  witness \(N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) together with an isomorphism
  \(e : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_X\). We take the pullback witness
  \((\mathtt{pullback}\,f).\mathrm{obj}\,N = f^*N\) and exhibit \(f^*M\) as
  \(\otimes\)-invertible by the composite
  \[
    (f^*M) \otimes_Y (f^*N)
      \;\xrightarrow[\sim]{\;(\text{\cref{lem:pullback_tensor_iso}})^{-1}\;}\;
    f^*(M \otimes_X N)
      \;\xrightarrow[\sim]{\;f^*e\;}\;
    f^*\mathcal{O}_X
      \;\xrightarrow[\sim]{\;\mathtt{pullbackUnitIso}\;}\;
    \mathcal{O}_Y,
  \]
  whose three arrows are, in order: the inverse of the general pullback--tensor
  isomorphism \cref{lem:pullback_tensor_iso}; the image
  \((\mathtt{pullback}\,f).\mathtt{mapIso}\,e\) of the invertibility witness \(e\) under
  the pullback functor; and the structure-sheaf comparison \(\mathtt{pullbackUnitIso}\)
  identifying \(f^*\mathcal{O}_X\) with \(\mathcal{O}_Y\) (\cref{lem:pullback_unit_iso}).
  Each is an isomorphism --- the first by the committed strong-monoidality of pullback,
  the second as the image of an isomorphism under a functor, the third by
  \cref{lem:pullback_unit_iso} --- so the composite is an isomorphism
  \(f^*M \otimes_Y f^*N \cong \mathcal{O}_Y\). Hence \(f^*N\) is a tensor inverse of
  \(f^*M\), and \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(f^*M)\) holds.
\end{proof}
